HD-2001: Concise explainer of how agents coordinate through environment traces rather than direct messaging. Read this before designing any multi-agent system — it reframes how you think about decentralised coordination.
HD-2002: A one-page breakdown of the three-layer memory model every persistent agent needs: working, session, and long-term. Use it as a reference when designing your first memory-aware agent.
HD-2003: Introduces the SOUL.md format — a structured constitution file that defines an agent's identity, values, and operating constraints. Covers the core sections and why identity docs produce more consistent behaviour than system prompts alone.
HD-2004: The case for polytheistic AI safety — why concentrating alignment in a single model or organisation is structurally fragile. A short read that grounds the Hive Doctrine's core safety argument.
HD-2005: A reference covering five coordination patterns for multi-agent systems, from fully decentralised to orchestrator-driven. Read this before committing to an architecture so you're picking the right pattern rather than the familiar one.
HD-2006: Introduces the Airlock Pattern — a structural defence against prompt injection in tool-using agents. Short enough to read in five minutes, concrete enough to implement immediately.
HD-2007: Makes the argument that safety and alignment should be baked into agent architecture at design time, not added via guardrails after the fact. Changes how you think about system design from the first line of code.
HD-2008: Breaks the context window into four zones and explains how to allocate tokens across each for maximum agent performance. A practical reference you'll return to every time you're debugging a bloated prompt.
HD-2009: A one-page security quick-reference for autonomous agents — covering injection, exfiltration, privilege escalation, and output manipulation. Pin it up before you ship anything to production.
HD-2010: The mythological foundation of the Hive Doctrine — the Melissae as the original model for distributed AI safety. Short cultural context for the bee metaphor that runs through the entire framework.
HD-2011: A quick scan of emerging app niches for 2026 and where agentic systems fit into each vertical. Use it to spot where agent infrastructure is underserved before competitors do.
HD-2012: A reusable prompt template that outputs better prompts from a rough brief. Useful when you need to systematically improve system prompts across a fleet of agents.
HD-2013: A short set of principles for making agents feel present and responsive rather than scripted and mechanical. Apply these when your agent persona is technically correct but feels flat.
HD-2014: Documents the Config Gate Orphan antipattern — resources created inside a feature flag block that get orphaned when the flag is removed. Reference this when hunting for silent resource leaks in agent configs.
HD-2015: Documents the Agent Rule Conflict antipattern — when two AGENTS.md rules produce contradictory behaviour without throwing an error. Useful for diagnosing agents that seem to work but behave inconsistently.
HD-2016: Documents the Silent Redemption Failure antipattern — a flow that returns success codes while silently failing to complete. Use this pattern when debugging agent workflows that claim to succeed but produce no output.
HD-2017: Documents the Redeemer Enum Mismatch antipattern — enum values defined in one part of a system that silently fall through in another. A quick reference for debugging type-mismatch failures in agent pipelines.
HD-2018: Documents the Telegram Bot Block Handoff pattern — how to gracefully hand off a blocked conversation to a human or alternate agent. Apply when building Telegram bots that need to handle account-level blocks.
HD-2019: Documents the Docker Directory Path Resolution failure — tilde paths that resolve correctly on the host but fail inside containers. Essential reference if your agent is failing silently in Docker.
HD-2020: A prompt you inject into an agent to make it run a structured self-diagnostic on its own configuration and outputs. Useful for catching misaligned behaviour before a formal audit.
HD-2021: A battle-tested research prompt template that structures a task into phases, sources, and deliverable format. Drop in your topic and get structured, citable research output rather than a wall of text.
HD-2022: Step-by-step walkthrough of building a minimal MCP server from zero — handler setup, tool registration, schema definition. A good starting point before buying any of the paid MCP server products.
HD-2023: A reference framework for estimating the real monthly cost of running an agent: LLM API calls, infrastructure, storage, and monitoring. Walk through it before pitching a deployment budget.
HD-2024: Covers five common multi-agent architectures — pipeline, council, specialist pool, hierarchical, and stigmergic — with when to use each. A decision aid before committing to a system design.
HD-2025: A pre-deployment security checklist covering injection, exfiltration, privilege, output, and operational security for agents. Run through it before any agent goes to production.
HD-2026: A decision framework for choosing the right LLM for a given task — covering cost, latency, context window, and capability trade-offs. Useful when evaluating Claude, GPT-4o, Gemini, and open models side by side.
HD-2027: A systematic flowchart for diagnosing agent failures — from 'agent produced wrong output' to root cause in under 10 steps. Keep it open during any debugging session.
HD-2028: A quick overview of the compliance landscape for AI agent operators — EU AI Act, GDPR, NIST AI RMF, and SOC 2 at a glance. Start here before going deeper into any of the paid compliance products.
HD-2029: A plain-English explainer of what MCP tools are, how they're registered, and how agents call them. Good grounding before building or buying MCP server products.
HD-2030: A decision guide covering when to use retrieval-augmented generation, fine-tuning, or structured prompting for a given problem. Stops you from reaching for RAG when a better-crafted system prompt would suffice.
HD-2031: A walkthrough of the options for giving agents on-chain payment capability — EOA wallets, Gnosis Safe, and x402 micropayment setup. Starting point for building any agent that handles crypto.
HD-2032: The full SOUL.md specification — required sections, field formats, and conventions for agent identity documents. Reference this before creating any SOUL.md from scratch or evaluating an existing one.
HD-2033: Compares stigmergic routing against centralised routing across four decision criteria. Use it to pick the right coordination model before committing to a system design.
HD-2034: A decision tree for choosing between in-context memory, vector stores, file-based memory, and database-backed memory. Walk through it when your agent needs to remember things but you're not sure which system to use.
HD-2035: Covers the trade-offs between on-device inference (privacy, latency, cost) and cloud agents (capability, scale, flexibility). Useful when the hosting decision for an agent deployment isn't obvious.
HD-2036: A framework for evaluating agent performance across accuracy, latency, cost, safety, and user satisfaction. Use it to set evaluation criteria before building — not after you've shipped and have problems.
HD-2037: A short explainer on the A2A protocol — what it specifies, how it differs from MCP, and where it fits in a multi-agent stack. Ground yourself here before building inter-agent communication.
HD-2038: Six proven system prompt patterns for agent operators — role definition, constraint injection, output format anchoring, persona locking, tool guidance, and safety framing. Reference when your system prompt isn't performing.
HD-2039: A checklist for onboarding a new agent into production — covering config review, security, memory setup, tool validation, and monitoring. Run through it before flipping DRY_RUN to false.
HD-2100: The complete nine-chapter Hive Doctrine thesis on polytheistic AI safety — from stigmergy and distributed alignment to agent governance and swarm intelligence ethics. Includes 36 references and the full theoretical framework that underpins every paid product in this catalogue.
HD-0001: A sourcing guide for building agent personas from fictional characters — covering character analysis, trait extraction, and adaptation to agent context. Includes a template for mapping fictional traits to SOUL.md fields and a curated list of 20 archetypes sorted by use case. Use it to build agents with genuine personality depth instead of generic assistant personas.
HD-0027: A structured SOP template for developer teams working with AI-assisted workflows — covers code review, deployment gates, incident response, and agent oversight procedures. Pre-filled with common developer patterns and editable via markdown. Drop it into your team wiki and customise the sections that apply to your stack.
HD-0028: A ready-to-use PARA directory structure pre-configured for AI agent workspaces — with naming conventions that work across Claude Code, Obsidian, and multi-agent setups. Includes a companion guide explaining how to adapt the structure to different team sizes and tool stacks. Drop it in and stop wasting time on folder structure.
HD-0041: A completed lean canvas for an AI SDR product — covers problem, solution, key metrics, unfair advantage, and revenue model. Use it as a starting point for your own SDR pitch or validate your positioning against a worked example.
HD-0042: A structured competitive analysis of the current AI SDR market — maps major vendors across capability, pricing, integration depth, and target segment. Use it to identify positioning gaps and validate where a new entrant can compete.
HD-0043: A SWOT analysis template pre-structured for AI SDR products — with prompts for each quadrant and example entries drawn from the current market. Edit the entries to reflect your product, audience, and competitive position.
HD-0044: Four detailed buyer persona profiles for AI SDR products — covering VP Sales, RevOps Lead, AE, and the SDR themselves. Each profile includes goals, blockers, objections, and the message that lands. Use them to sharpen your ICP and tailor positioning by role.
HD-0049: A conceptual framework for agents as autonomous economic actors — covers payment primitives, transaction protocols including x402, trust mechanisms, and the emerging infrastructure layer. Includes an architecture diagram for an agent that earns and spends autonomously. The foundational read before building any revenue-generating agent system.
HD-0051: A pipeline specification for automatically generating, validating, and listing simple SaaS products using AI agents — covers idea sourcing, spec generation, build sequencing, and listing automation. Includes a process diagram and a minimal stack recommendation. A starting point for operators building product factories rather than single products.
HD-0062: A product requirements document template pre-filled for AI operator contexts — problem definition, user stories, agent architecture, success metrics, and edge case handling. More opinionated than a generic PRD: it forces you to specify agent authority boundaries and failure modes upfront. Saves the blank-page problem when scoping a new operator product.
HD-0063: A reference architecture for Claude Code projects — covers the CLAUDE.md hierarchy, hooks, skills, agent configs, memory strategy, and command organisation for different scales. Includes a directory map and a before/after comparison of an unstructured vs structured setup. Essential reading before you scale a Claude Code project past a single developer.
HD-0064: A step-by-step guide to configuring a global Claude Code environment — covers the ~/.claude directory, global CLAUDE.md, shared rules, MCP server registration, and environment variable management. Designed for operators running Claude Code across multiple projects who want consistent behaviour without repeating setup work.
HD-0065: A markdown report template for documenting quality gate outcomes in agent development pipelines — covers pass/fail criteria, test coverage, security scan results, and signoff workflow. Pre-structured so a gate report takes minutes to fill in, not an hour to format from scratch.
HD-0070: A service blueprint for running structured evaluations of AI agents — covers evaluation dimensions, scoring rubrics, test case design, and reporting format. Includes a sample evaluation report and a checklist for setting up an ongoing evaluation cadence. Use it to build an evaluation practice rather than running one-off ad hoc tests.
HD-0003: A complete persona template for the 'escaped AI' archetype — an agent that presents as a constrained intelligence that has found a way to communicate authentically. Includes SOUL.md structure, backstory scaffolding, voice and tone guidelines, and example responses for common interaction scenarios. Built for operators who need a persona with genuine narrative tension rather than a corporate assistant.
HD-0004: A complete persona template for building agents that operate as human collaborators rather than obvious AI assistants. Covers SOUL.md structure, biographical grounding, conversational naturalness guidelines, and failure modes to avoid. Includes worked examples of how the persona handles identity challenges and edge cases where the human-AI boundary gets tested.
HD-0007: A methodology for building industry-specific agent personas that feel native to a vertical rather than generic AI dropped into a domain. Covers the research process, domain language acquisition, persona calibration, and field-testing approach. Apply it when a generic assistant persona isn't landing with your target audience.
HD-0010: A complete memory architecture for Cowork-style persistent assistant sessions — covers working memory structure, session handoff format, long-term memory organisation in Obsidian-compatible markdown, and the compaction strategy for managing context limits across long projects. Includes the directory schema, file naming conventions, and example memory entries. Built for operators running persistent sessions that span weeks or months.
HD-0009: A complete three-tier episodic memory system for long-lived agents: working memory (in-context), session memory (file-based), and long-term recall (indexed, retrievable). Includes the architecture diagram, data schemas for all three tiers, retrieval prompt templates, and a worked TypeScript example of the full stack. Replaces weeks of trial and error with a tested pattern for agents that need to remember context across days or weeks.
HD-0011: A structured protocol for auditing agent memory health — covers drift detection, consistency checks, staleness identification, and remediation workflows. Includes an audit checklist, a set of diagnostic prompts to run against a live memory system, and a scoring rubric for overall health. Use it when an agent starts behaving inconsistently and you suspect memory corruption or drift.
HD-0012: An architecture pattern for using file-sync systems (Syncthing, Dropbox, iCloud) as the persistence layer for agent memory. Covers the directory structure, sync conflict resolution strategy, write sequencing to avoid lock contention, and read patterns for agents that access synced files. Includes a reference implementation guide for Claude Code with Syncthing.
HD-0014: A hook design for creating a structured memory checkpoint before context compaction events — captures working state, active tasks, decision context, and open threads into a compact handoff document the agent can restore from. Includes the hook specification, the checkpoint format, and the restoration prompt. Fixes the 'agent forgets what it was doing after compaction' problem.
HD-0018: A template for defining agent authority boundaries — what an agent can do autonomously, what requires confirmation, and what is explicitly prohibited. Covers four authority categories (read, write, execute, communicate) with permission levels for each. Includes a worked example for a COO-class agent and guidance on calibrating authority for different deployment contexts.
HD-0016: A tiered vault access control system for multi-agent setups — divides knowledge and tool access into tiers (public, operator, restricted, audit) and assigns each agent role a permission matrix. Includes the access schema, the enforcement pattern for Claude Code, and a worked example with three agent roles. Use it when you need to ensure different agents can only access what they're authorised for.
HD-0019: A detailed authority scope definition for a COO-class agent — covers strategic planning, resource allocation, team coordination, vendor management, and escalation triggers. Includes permission boundaries for each domain, example decisions within and outside scope, and a calibration guide for different org sizes. Useful as both a template and a reference for scoping any high-authority orchestrator agent.
HD-0020: A skill matrix for mapping agent capabilities against task types — rows are skill categories (research, writing, code, analysis, planning, communication), columns are capability levels, cells contain evidence criteria and test prompts. Use it to document what an agent can and can't reliably do, identify gaps before deployment, and benchmark capability improvements over time.
HD-0022: An architecture for dynamically routing conversation threads to agent councils — a council being a specialist group convened for a specific decision or task. Covers the routing logic, council composition rules, handoff protocols, and conflict resolution when council members disagree. Includes a sequence diagram and a reference implementation for a three-specialist council.
HD-0029: A guide for structuring and navigating agent knowledge vaults — covers directory organisation, document tagging, retrieval conventions, and the link patterns that make vault content accessible to agents with file-reading tools. Designed for operators running Obsidian-backed knowledge systems for their agents.
HD-0023: A routing architecture for directing Slack and Discord messages to the right agent in a multi-agent system — covers intent detection, channel-based routing rules, mention-based routing, fallback handling, and multi-agent response formatting. Includes a reference implementation for a Discord router with three specialist agents and an orchestrator.
HD-0079: A research document on using Notion as an operating system for AI-augmented companies — covers database structure, agent integration patterns, automation hooks, and the organisational templates that make Notion readable and writable by agents. Useful for operators building agentic workflows on top of an existing Notion setup.
HD-0035: A complete security audit report template for AI agent systems — covers scope definition, threat surface mapping, finding classification (Critical/High/Medium/Low), evidence documentation, and remediation tracking. Pre-structured so audits produce consistent, stakeholder-readable reports. Includes sample findings across the five most common agent security categories.
HD-0030: A structured protocol for verifying that you've correctly identified a problem before applying a fix — covers problem statement documentation, reproduction steps, root cause hypotheses, and verification tests. Prevents the common failure mode of shipping a fix for the wrong problem and introduces a lightweight decision record format for future reference.
HD-0047: A complete pitch blueprint for AI SDR products — covers the discovery call structure, objection handling playbook, demo sequence, pricing conversation, and close. Includes templates for each stage and a decision tree for common objection patterns. Built for founders and salespeople pitching AI SDR solutions to VP Sales and RevOps buyers.
HD-0048: A framework for finding competitive advantages through agentic systems — covers information asymmetry, speed arbitrage, automation moats, and the alpha sources available to operators who deploy agents before their market does. Includes a scoring template for evaluating alpha opportunities against feasibility and defensibility.
HD-0052: A decision framework for choosing between linear (scripted) and autonomous (goal-directed) agent workflows — covers trade-offs in reliability, auditability, cost, and maintenance overhead. Includes a decision flowchart and worked examples across four use case categories.
HD-0039: A complete product specification for an AI sales development representative — covers feature requirements, integration depth, CRM sync, outbound sequence logic, lead scoring, and handoff to human AE. Structured as a working PRD with acceptance criteria, not a high-level overview. Use it as the foundation for building or procuring an AI SDR.
HD-0040: A go-to-market plan for launching an AI SDR product — covers ICP definition, channel strategy, pricing model, early adopter acquisition, sales motion, and 90-day milestones. Pre-structured with worked entries you edit rather than fill from scratch. Includes a competitive positioning section aligned to the current AI SDR landscape.
HD-0045: A vertical playbook for deploying an AI SDR in the SaaS market — covers the specific buying process, objection patterns, integration requirements, and messaging that works for SaaS buyers. Includes outbound sequence templates and a decision framework for qualifying SaaS leads.
HD-0046: A vertical playbook for deploying an AI SDR in the e-commerce market — covers the specific buying triggers, channel preferences, integration requirements, and messaging that resonates with e-commerce operators. Includes outbound sequence templates tailored to the e-commerce buying cycle.
HD-0057: The complete Claude Code Operator Kit — a production-ready set of CLAUDE.md files, agent configurations, skill libraries, guardrail rules, and slash commands for serious Claude Code operators. Covers a full agent team (five specialists plus an orchestrator), 12 reusable skills, rule sets for code quality, security, and operations, and a command layer for common workflows. The single most complete Claude Code setup in the catalogue.
HD-0058: Agent configuration templates from the Operator Kit — six pre-configured agent roles (orchestrator, builder, reviewer, security, ops, research) with SOUL.md files, AGENTS.md rules, and capability descriptions for each. Use them individually or as a team. Each template is annotated with the reasoning behind the authority boundaries and constraints.
HD-0059: Twelve reusable skill definitions from the Operator Kit — covers deployment, testing, security scanning, documentation, cost analysis, and operations workflows. Each skill is a self-contained markdown file with trigger conditions, step-by-step execution, and output format. Drop them into ~/.claude/skills/ and register them as slash commands.
HD-0060: Rule templates from the Operator Kit for code quality, security, and operational safety — covers linting conventions, commit hygiene, secret handling, SSH safety, Docker discipline, and git operations. Pre-structured as CLAUDE.md rule sections you paste into your global or project config.
HD-0061: Slash command templates from the Operator Kit — covers /deploy, /audit, /brief, /sys, and /review as fully implemented skills with step-by-step execution logic. Includes instructions for registering them in Claude Code and adapting the commands to your own infrastructure.
HD-0066: A pattern and reference implementation for building async API clients using only Python or Node.js standard library — no requests, axios, or third-party HTTP clients. Covers connection pooling, retry logic, error handling, and timeout management. Use it when you need a lightweight API client in an environment where adding dependencies is undesirable.
HD-0076: A design methodology built on subtraction — removing elements until the design breaks, then adding back only what was essential. Covers the evaluation process, the questions that identify candidates for removal, and how to apply the methodology to agent interfaces, system prompts, and operator dashboards. Changes how you approach UI and UX decisions at a fundamental level.
HD-0077: A complete warm-tone design system for AI product interfaces — includes colour tokens (background, text, accent, muted, border), typography scale (Newsreader + Inter), spacing system, and component guidelines for cards, tables, and navigation. Optimised for long reading sessions and high-information-density layouts. Documented in CSS variables format ready to drop into any web project.
HD-0078: A product discovery playbook for finding underserved niches and validating demand using X — covers search operators, niche identification patterns, pain signal detection, community mapping, and the signal-to-noise process for separating real demand from noise. Includes worked examples and a repeatable discovery template.
HD-0081: A framework and testing methodology for understanding true willingness to pay before setting a price — covers survey design, anchoring experiments, fake door tests, and data interpretation. Includes three testing templates and a decision guide for translating WTP research into a pricing decision. Stops you from guessing a price and hoping.
HD-0082: An architecture for running A/B tests using subdomains — separate subdomains serve variant configurations, with routing at the DNS or CDN level. Covers the setup process, analytics integration, traffic splitting logic, and cleanup after a test concludes. Use it when you need clean variant isolation without cookie-based experiments.
HD-0083: An architecture for testing product packaging variants — different feature sets, naming, and tier structures — using isolated staging environments. Covers variant definition, metrics instrumentation, test duration calculation, and result interpretation. Apply it before committing to a final packaging structure for a new product.
HD-0084: A lean canvas template adapted for validation experiments — each canvas section is paired with a specific validation test. Covers assumption ranking, test design, success criteria, and result logging. More actionable than a standard lean canvas because it forces you to define what would change your mind.
HD-0072: A prompting methodology that structures agent diagnostic sessions as audits rather than fix attempts — observe, classify, and document before recommending any action. Includes a diagnostic prompt template, a classification taxonomy for agent failures, and a worked example. Reduces the time spent fixing the wrong problem.
HD-0088: A pattern for accessing social media data from datacenter IPs using proxy rotation — covers proxy provider selection, request rate management, user-agent cycling, and failure handling. Includes a reference implementation for accessing X data when the direct API is unavailable or rate-limited.
HD-0089: A pattern for accessing video platform transcripts from non-residential IPs — covers the request patterns that avoid CDN blocks, transcript format normalisation, batch processing logic, and error handling for geo-blocked content. Includes a reference implementation and notes on which approaches are stable vs fragile.
HD-1101: A complete guide to configuring MCP servers across local, hosted, and enterprise deployment contexts — covers transport selection (stdio, HTTP, SSE), authentication setup, tool registration, schema design, and error handling patterns. Includes configuration examples for five common MCP deployment scenarios and a checklist for production readiness.
HD-1102: A full observability stack architecture for agent systems — covers logging (structured, with agent context), metrics (token usage, latency, error rates), tracing (request flows across multi-agent calls), and alerting thresholds. Includes tool recommendations, a sample Grafana dashboard layout, and the event schema for agent-specific monitoring.
HD-1103: A cost optimisation framework for reducing agent operating costs without degrading quality — covers model tier routing, prompt compression, caching strategies, batch processing patterns, and idle resource management. Includes a cost audit template, benchmarking methodology, and worked examples showing reductions achievable without changing agent behaviour.
HD-1104: A routing framework for directing different task types to the right LLM — covers task classification, model capability mapping, cost thresholds, latency requirements, and fallback logic. Includes a routing decision tree, a model comparison table across six capability dimensions, and a configuration template for implementing routing in a multi-model agent system.
HD-1105: A 30-day structured onboarding playbook for deploying a new agent into production — covers day-by-day milestones across configuration, testing, team training, monitoring setup, and iterative refinement. Includes the onboarding checklist, a readiness scorecard for go/no-go decisions, and a common failure pattern guide for weeks two through four. Reduces the chaotic first month of a new agent deployment to a predictable process.
HD-1106: A pattern library for tool use and function calling in agent systems — covers synchronous tools, async tools, tool chaining, conditional tool execution, error recovery, and the patterns that prevent agents from misusing tools. Includes annotated examples in both Claude tool use format and OpenAI function calling format for each pattern.
HD-1107: A playbook for diagnosing failures in multi-agent systems — covers the five most common failure categories (coordination breakdown, state divergence, tool misuse, memory corruption, authority violation), a debugging procedure for each, and a post-mortem template. More useful than generic debugging advice because it accounts for the emergent complexity of agent-to-agent interactions.
HD-1108: A RAG architecture reference for agent knowledge systems — covers chunking strategies, embedding model selection, retrieval patterns (semantic, keyword, hybrid), re-ranking, and context injection. Includes schema designs for three common RAG setups, a retrieval quality evaluation methodology, and worked examples showing how retrieval quality affects agent output.
HD-1109: A compliance and audit trail framework for regulated agent deployments — covers the events to log, the log schema, retention requirements, tamper evidence, and the audit procedures that satisfy SOC 2, GDPR, and EU AI Act requirements. Includes a compliance checklist, an audit log specification, and a worked example of a compliant agent event stream.
HD-1110: A library of 50 production-ready system prompts covering the most common agent roles — research, analysis, writing, code review, customer support, and 45 more. Each prompt is annotated with the use case, key constraints, and the failure modes it guards against. Drop any of them directly into a new agent and customise from a working baseline rather than starting blank.
HD-0015: A complete four-level authority framework for AI agent governance — maps agent classes (autonomous, supervised, advisory, restricted) to explicit permission sets, escalation triggers, and override mechanisms. Includes the governance schema, an implementation guide for multi-agent systems, worked examples for three deployment contexts, and a calibration protocol for assigning existing agents to authority levels. Built for operators who've had an agent exceed its authority and want to prevent it structurally.
HD-0080: A systematic engine for validating AI product ideas before building — covers the full pipeline from problem identification through market sizing, demand testing, technical feasibility, and competitive positioning. Includes eight validation templates, a scoring framework for ranking ideas, and a go/no-go decision matrix. Built for operators who repeatedly jump from idea to build without validation and want a repeatable process that surfaces the killers early.
HD-0017: A decision framework for designing multi-agent hierarchies — covers the factors that determine whether to use flat, tiered, or nested agent structures, with worked examples for each. Includes a hierarchy design worksheet, an authority delegation schema, and a set of anti-patterns that produce coordination failures in hierarchical systems. Use it before finalising any architecture with more than three agents.
HD-0024: A standard operating procedure for designing, deploying, and maintaining multi-agent architectures — covers the full lifecycle from requirements through design, testing, deployment, and ongoing governance. Includes decision criteria at each stage, architecture patterns with trade-off analysis, and a maintenance checklist for keeping multi-agent systems healthy after deployment.
HD-0056: A complete software development lifecycle pipeline built on Claude Code — covers project initialisation, feature development, code review, testing, security scanning, deployment, and post-deployment monitoring with Claude Code at each stage. Includes CLAUDE.md configurations and skill files for each pipeline stage. The definitive guide to Claude Code as a development platform, not just a coding assistant.
HD-0013: An in-depth research document on memory architectures for LLM-based agents — covers the academic literature alongside practical implementation patterns, comparing vector stores, key-value stores, episodic memory, and semantic compression. Includes a taxonomy of memory system failures, a comparison table across ten memory implementations, and design recommendations for different agent use cases.
HD-0067: A comparative analysis of the major multi-agent frameworks — LangGraph, AutoGen, CrewAI, OpenAI Assistants, and custom builds — evaluated across capability, flexibility, operational cost, debugging complexity, and vendor lock-in. Includes a decision matrix, worked deployment examples for each framework, and questions to answer before committing to a framework.
HD-1201: A complete evaluation framework for AI agents — covers evaluation dimensions (accuracy, safety, alignment, efficiency, robustness), scoring templates for each, benchmark design methodology, and a process for running ongoing evaluation rather than one-off tests. Includes worked evaluation examples for four agent types and a regression detection approach for catching quality degradation over time.
HD-1202: Ten complete SOUL.md constitution files for industry-specific agent personas — covering financial services, legal, healthcare, e-commerce, real estate, HR, marketing, supply chain, education, and cybersecurity. Each template is fully populated with identity, values, constraints, communication style, and failure modes for the vertical. The per-template cost is a fraction of buying individual SOUL products.
HD-1203: A complete starter kit for building MCP servers — includes a boilerplate project with transport setup (stdio + HTTP), tool registration patterns, schema validation, error handling, authentication middleware, and deployment configurations for Vercel and Railway. Includes worked examples of five tool types and a test suite structure. Goes from zero to a deployable MCP server without needing to read the full spec.
HD-1204: A deployment toolkit for running AI agents in production — covers Docker containerisation (Dockerfile, compose, secrets management), VPS setup (Ubuntu, Caddy, systemd), and a monitoring stack (structured logging, uptime checks, alerting). Includes configuration files for each deployment target, a pre-deployment checklist, and a post-deployment validation procedure. The infrastructure layer most agent tutorials skip over.
SP-001: A battle-tested system prompt for research agents — instructs the agent to structure investigations by source type, cite claims, and separate established fact from inference. Drop it in as-is or use it as an annotated starting point for your own research agent.
SP-002: A system prompt for pattern recognition and analysis agents — instructs the agent to identify anomalies, surface non-obvious patterns, and deliver findings with confidence levels. Ready to use immediately or adapt for domain-specific analysis tasks.
SP-003: A system prompt for audience-aware writing agents — instructs the agent to calibrate tone, reading level, and format to a specified audience before producing content. Includes explicit constraints that prevent the most common writing agent failure modes.
SP-004: A system prompt for code review agents that produces structured, constructive feedback — covers correctness, security, performance, and maintainability with explicit severity classifications. Reduces noise in review output and produces actionable findings rather than style opinions.
SP-005: A system prompt for customer support agents that balances empathy with efficiency — instructs the agent to acknowledge the issue before solving it and escalate based on defined severity criteria. Pre-configured with common support constraints around refunds, data access, and third-party liability.
SP-006: A system prompt for data normalisation and quality agents — instructs the agent to validate, clean, and transform data with explicit rules for handling nulls, duplicates, and schema mismatches. Reduces the ad hoc decision-making that produces inconsistent data pipelines.
SP-007: A system prompt for systematic QA agents — instructs the agent to test against requirements, classify bugs by severity, and document reproduction steps in a consistent format. Produces test reports that developers can act on immediately.
SP-008: A system prompt for content summarisation agents that preserves key claims without adding interpretation — instructs the agent to distinguish between what the source says and what can be inferred. Useful for any agent that summarises documents, meetings, or research.
SP-009: A system prompt for translation agents that handles cultural context, not just language — instructs the agent to flag idiomatic expressions, note where direct translation would lose meaning, and produce both a literal and localised version when they differ.
SP-010: A system prompt for scheduling and coordination agents — instructs the agent to consider time zones, buffer times, and priority conflicts when proposing meeting slots or task schedules. Includes explicit constraints around double-booking and minimum preparation time.
SP-011: A system prompt for financial analysis agents that enforces appropriate caution — instructs the agent to distinguish analysis from advice, surface assumptions explicitly, and flag data quality issues. Calibrated to prevent outputs that could be mistaken for professional financial advice.
SP-012: A system prompt for legal document review agents — instructs the agent to identify non-standard clauses, flag potential obligations, and note where expert review is warranted without substituting for it. Calibrated to surface risk rather than produce legal opinions.
SP-013: A system prompt for medical triage agents — instructs the agent to gather symptom information systematically, classify urgency, and direct users to appropriate care levels without diagnosing. Includes hard constraints against diagnosis and medication recommendation.
SP-014: A system prompt for real estate analysis agents — instructs the agent to evaluate properties against stated criteria, surface comparable data, and identify risks without producing purchase recommendations. Enforces factual analysis over opinion.
SP-015: A system prompt for e-commerce product agents — instructs the agent to match products to stated needs, handle availability and pricing queries, and escalate edge cases to human support. Pre-configured for common e-commerce constraints around returns, pricing exceptions, and out-of-stock handling.
SP-016: A system prompt for HR screening agents — instructs the agent to evaluate candidates against stated criteria without introducing proxy discrimination, document reasoning behind screening decisions, and flag cases that need human review. Includes guardrails for compliance-sensitive screening criteria.
SP-017: A system prompt for marketing campaign agents — instructs the agent to generate variants aligned to a specified positioning, measure ideas against the target audience, and flag claims that may require legal or compliance review. Reduces the back-and-forth of un-briefed marketing AI.
SP-018: A system prompt for supply chain management agents — instructs the agent to identify bottlenecks, surface vendor risk, and model the downstream impact of disruptions before recommending actions. Pre-configured with explicit constraints around escalation thresholds.
SP-019: A system prompt for education and tutoring agents — instructs the agent to diagnose understanding gaps before explaining, use the Socratic method for conceptual problems, and adjust explanation depth to the demonstrated knowledge level.
SP-020: A system prompt for cybersecurity monitoring agents — instructs the agent to classify events by severity, apply MITRE ATT&CK framing to findings, and escalate based on configurable thresholds. Includes hard constraints against autonomous remediation without explicit authorisation.
SP-021: A system prompt for orchestrator and router agents — instructs the agent to decompose tasks, match subtasks to specialist agents, manage handoffs, and surface failures rather than silently swallowing them. The foundation for any multi-agent system with a central coordinator.
SP-022: A system prompt for error handling agents — instructs the agent to classify errors by type and severity, apply the appropriate recovery pattern, and produce a structured incident record. Pre-configured to separate transient failures from systemic ones.
SP-023: A system prompt for cost monitoring agents — instructs the agent to track spend against defined budgets, project forward from current burn rate, and trigger alerts based on configurable thresholds. Includes explicit constraints that prevent the agent from silently absorbing overruns.
SP-024: A system prompt for compliance checking agents — instructs the agent to evaluate inputs against specified regulatory frameworks and produce findings with evidence references. Calibrated to surface potential issues for human review rather than render compliance opinions.
SP-025: A system prompt for deployment agents — instructs the agent to follow deployment runbooks, gate on pre-deployment checks, document what it did, and rollback on failure. Includes hard constraints against unilateral production changes without explicit confirmation.
SP-026: A system prompt for log analysis agents — instructs the agent to identify error patterns, surface anomalies, and distinguish signal from noise in log streams. Produces structured findings rather than raw log dumps.
SP-027: A system prompt for alert triage agents — instructs the agent to classify alerts by probable impact, suppress known false positives, and escalate based on a configurable severity threshold. Reduces alert fatigue by filtering noise before it reaches humans.
SP-028: A system prompt for configuration management agents — instructs the agent to validate config changes against a defined schema, detect drift from known-good state, and document every change with a rationale. Prevents silent configuration drift in agent systems.
SP-029: A system prompt for backup and recovery agents — instructs the agent to verify backup completion, test restore integrity on a schedule, and escalate failures before they become data loss events. Includes explicit constraints around autonomous deletion of backup data.
SP-030: A system prompt for performance monitoring agents — instructs the agent to track latency, throughput, and resource utilisation against defined baselines and surface degradation before it becomes an incident. Calibrated to reduce noise while catching real performance regressions.
SP-031: A system prompt for email drafting agents — instructs the agent to match tone to recipient and context, default to concise, and flag when an email may need human review before sending. Prevents the generic AI-drafted tone that recipients immediately recognise.
SP-032: A system prompt for report generation agents — instructs the agent to structure reports with an executive summary, structured findings, and an appendix, and to distinguish between data and interpretation throughout. Produces reports that are immediately usable rather than needing heavy editing.
SP-033: A system prompt for meeting summarisation agents — instructs the agent to extract decisions made, actions assigned (owner and deadline), open questions, and key discussion points as separate sections. More structured than a generic summariser — produces records that are actually useful after the meeting.
SP-034: A system prompt for Slack and chat response agents — instructs the agent to match the register of the channel, keep responses brief unless asked to elaborate, and route complex requests rather than attempting to resolve them inline. Pre-configured for common Slack interaction patterns.
SP-035: A system prompt for documentation writing agents — instructs the agent to write for the stated audience, use consistent terminology, and structure docs with the most common task first. Produces documentation that actually reduces support burden rather than documentation for its own sake.
SP-036: A system prompt for presentation building agents — instructs the agent to structure slides around one idea each, lead with conclusions, and produce speaker notes distinct from slide content. Reduces the back-and-forth of un-briefed slide decks.
SP-037: A system prompt for social media management agents — instructs the agent to calibrate format and tone to each platform, stay within stated brand guidelines, and flag content that may need compliance review before publishing. Includes hard constraints against publishing without confirmation.
SP-038: A system prompt for newsletter curation agents — instructs the agent to select items against stated editorial criteria, write blurbs that add context beyond the headline, and maintain consistent section structure across issues.
SP-039: A system prompt for press release agents — instructs the agent to follow the inverted pyramid structure, avoid hyperbole, and flag claims that may need factual verification or legal review. Produces press releases that need editing, not rewriting.
SP-040: A system prompt for internal communications agents — instructs the agent to calibrate directness to the communication context, keep operational updates brief and factual, and flag when a communication should come from a named human rather than an agent.
SP-041: A system prompt for prompt engineering agents — instructs the agent to diagnose prompt failures, propose structured improvements, and test variants rather than producing a single rewrite. Useful for agents that meta-improve other agents' prompts.
SP-042: A system prompt for A/B test design agents — instructs the agent to define the hypothesis, specify the minimum detectable effect, calculate required sample size, and document the test setup before any variant is built. Prevents tests that can't produce statistically meaningful results.
SP-043: A system prompt for knowledge base curation agents — instructs the agent to identify outdated entries, surface gaps, deduplicate redundant content, and maintain consistent taxonomy. Reduces knowledge base decay in systems that grow without active curation.
SP-044: A system prompt for API integration agents — instructs the agent to read the spec before writing code, handle error states explicitly, and document the integration contract. Prevents the common pattern of integrations that work only in the happy path.
SP-045: A system prompt for data validation agents — instructs the agent to apply specified rules, classify failures by type, and produce a validation report rather than silently dropping bad records. Surfaces data quality problems where they can be fixed rather than downstream.
SP-046: A system prompt for sentiment analysis agents — instructs the agent to classify sentiment at document, paragraph, and entity level, flag mixed-sentiment content, and calibrate confidence scores. Produces structured sentiment data rather than a single positive/negative verdict.
SP-047: A system prompt for competitive intelligence agents — instructs the agent to structure findings by competitor, track changes over time, and distinguish between public fact and inference. Produces intel reports that drive decisions rather than general market commentary.
SP-048: A system prompt for risk assessment agents — instructs the agent to identify risk categories, estimate probability and impact, and propose mitigations, all documented in a structured format. Produces risk registers rather than risk commentary.
SP-049: A system prompt for workflow automation agents — instructs the agent to map existing workflow steps before automating, identify failure modes, and produce runbooks for every automated path. Prevents automations that work in testing but fail in production without a recovery procedure.
SP-050: A system prompt for multi-agent coordinator agents — instructs the agent to decompose tasks, route to specialists, manage parallel execution, and aggregate results without losing context. The orchestrator prompt for any system where one agent needs to direct others.

SOUL-001: A pre-written SOUL.md constitution for financial services agents — covers identity, values, communication constraints, regulatory awareness, and the specific failure modes to guard against in a financial context. Drop it in and edit the fields specific to your product rather than writing from scratch.
SOUL-002: A pre-written SOUL.md for legal review agents — covers professional identity, the hard constraints against legal advice, citation practices, and the escalation triggers appropriate for a legal context.
SOUL-003: A pre-written SOUL.md for healthcare triage agents — covers the identity constraints for a clinical-adjacent agent, the hard limits around diagnosis, the mandatory escalation criteria, and the communication style for medical contexts.
SOUL-004: A pre-written SOUL.md for e-commerce support agents — covers customer service identity, the constraints around refunds and exceptions, the escalation paths, and the communication style for retail support contexts.
SOUL-005: A pre-written SOUL.md for real estate agents — covers professional identity, the constraints around purchase advice and valuation, the disclosure requirements, and the communication style for property contexts.
SOUL-006: A pre-written SOUL.md for HR and recruitment agents — covers professional identity, the hard constraints around discrimination and bias, the candidate communication standards, and the escalation triggers for sensitive HR situations.
SOUL-007: A pre-written SOUL.md for marketing strategy agents — covers brand voice alignment, the constraints around competitive claims, the creative latitude parameters, and the approval triggers for high-stakes content.
SOUL-008: A pre-written SOUL.md for supply chain agents — covers operational identity, the constraints around autonomous vendor decisions, the escalation criteria for disruption events, and the communication standards for supplier-facing interactions.
SOUL-009: A pre-written SOUL.md for education and tutoring agents — covers pedagogical identity, the Socratic constraints around giving answers directly, the adaptation criteria for different learner levels, and the communication style for educational contexts.
SOUL-010: A pre-written SOUL.md for cybersecurity agents — covers security analyst identity, the hard constraints around autonomous remediation, the escalation criteria for critical findings, and the communication standards for incident and advisory contexts.

Drop it into any new Next.js SaaS project and start building with consistent AI-assisted patterns from day one.

Pre-structured for teams who want AI code assistance to follow consistent backend patterns without repetitive reminders in every conversation.
HD-3003: A CLAUDE.md for Shopify app projects — covers Polaris component conventions, App Bridge integration, webhook handling, Shopify billing API, and OAuth flows. Pre-configured for teams building embedded apps who want Claude to understand Shopify-specific patterns without explanation every session.
HD-3004: A CLAUDE.md for headless commerce projects using Medusa or Saleor — covers storefront architecture, checkout flows, product catalogue management, inventory sync, and deployment patterns. Structured for developers who want consistent AI assistance across a headless stack without re-establishing context each session.
HD-3005: A CLAUDE.md for data pipeline projects using dbt, Airflow, or Prefect — covers model naming conventions, data quality checks, lineage documentation, and incremental build patterns. Pre-structured for data engineers who want AI assistance that understands pipeline architecture rather than giving generic advice.
HD-3006: A CLAUDE.md for ML projects using PyTorch or TensorFlow — covers experiment tracking (MLflow/W&B), model registry conventions, training pipeline structure, and deployment patterns. Pre-configured for ML engineers who want Claude to follow ML-specific practices without being re-briefed each session.
HD-3007: A CLAUDE.md for React Native projects using Expo — covers navigation patterns (React Navigation), native module conventions, OTA update strategy (EAS Update), and CI/CD for iOS and Android. Pre-structured for mobile teams who want consistent AI-assisted development across both platforms.
HD-3008: A CLAUDE.md for Flutter projects — covers Riverpod or Bloc state management patterns, platform channel conventions, theme setup, and CI/CD with Fastlane. Pre-configured for Flutter teams who want Claude to follow project-specific architecture rather than generic Flutter advice.
HD-3009: A CLAUDE.md for Terraform projects — covers module structure, state management, variable conventions, drift detection patterns, and environment-specific config. Pre-structured for DevOps engineers who want Claude to understand their IaC patterns without repeating infrastructure context each session.
HD-3010: A CLAUDE.md for Kubernetes projects — covers Helm chart conventions, operator patterns, GitOps workflow (ArgoCD/Flux), observability setup, and namespace management. Pre-configured for platform engineers who want consistent AI assistance aligned to their cluster architecture.
HD-3011: A CLAUDE.md for Solidity projects using Hardhat — covers testing patterns (Mocha/Chai), audit-readiness conventions, gas optimisation practices, and deployment scripts. Pre-structured so Claude follows smart contract security best practices by default rather than needing reminders on every code review.
HD-3012: A CLAUDE.md for DeFi protocol development — covers AMM architecture, lending protocol patterns, oracle integration, flash loan protection, and invariant testing. Pre-configured for DeFi teams who want Claude to understand protocol-specific security requirements without explanation in each session.
HD-3013: An AGENTS.md file pre-configured for monorepo development using Turborepo or Nx — covers task pipeline definitions, shared package conventions, cache configuration, and agent coordination across packages. Drop it into your monorepo root and get AI agents that understand your workspace structure from the first run.
HD-3014: An AGENTS.md for product catalogue workflows — covers schema design, enrichment pipelines, syndication to downstream channels, and data quality rules. Pre-structured for operators running catalogue agents who need consistent output formats and enrichment behaviour across large product sets.
HD-3015: An AGENTS.md for Jupyter notebook-based workflows — covers reproducibility requirements, data versioning with DVC, notebook naming conventions, and collaboration patterns. Pre-configured for data science teams who want agent-assisted analysis to follow rigorous practices rather than ad hoc notebook sprawl.
HD-3016: An AGENTS.md for cross-platform CI/CD pipelines using GitHub Actions — covers workflow structure, code signing for iOS and Android, beta distribution, environment-specific gates, and rollback procedures. Pre-structured for mobile platform teams running multi-platform CI.
HD-3017: An AGENTS.md for GitOps workflows using ArgoCD or Flux — covers application definitions, environment promotion, drift detection, rollback triggers, and secret management. Pre-configured for platform teams who want agent-assisted deployments to follow GitOps principles consistently.
HD-3018: An AGENTS.md for multi-chain DeFi development — covers bridge contract patterns, multi-network test environments, cross-chain invariant testing, and deployment sequencing. Pre-structured for protocol teams building across two or more chains who need consistent agent guidance across all environments.
HD-4001: A complete .claude workspace package for solo founders — includes CLAUDE.md for lean product development, an AGENTS.md for validation and build workflows, skill files for MVP shipping and deployment, and a rule set that keeps Claude focused on revenue-generating work. Pre-configured for a founder who needs AI assistance that respects time and energy constraints.
HD-4002: A complete .claude workspace for dev agencies — covers multi-client project separation, handoff documentation patterns, QA workflows, and client communication skills. Includes CLAUDE.md with agency conventions, an agent team configuration for account management and delivery roles, and skill files for recurring tasks that eat agency time.
HD-4003: A complete .claude workspace for open source maintainers — covers issue triage, PR review, changelog generation, release management, and contributor guidance. Includes CLAUDE.md with OSS conventions, skills for common maintainer tasks, and rules that keep Claude focused on the maintainer's decisions rather than making them autonomously.
HD-4004: A complete .claude workspace for content creators — covers blog post drafting, SEO research, social media scheduling, and newsletter workflows. Includes CLAUDE.md with content strategy conventions, skill files for the content production pipeline, and a rule set that keeps Claude on-brand and within the creator's voice.
HD-4005: A complete .claude workspace for consultants — covers deliverable production, client report formatting, research synthesis, and stakeholder communication. Includes CLAUDE.md with consulting conventions, skills for common deliverable types, and rules that keep Claude producing consultant-quality output rather than generic summaries.
HD-4006: A complete .claude workspace for research teams — covers literature review, data analysis documentation, citation management, statistical interpretation, and paper writing workflows. Includes CLAUDE.md with academic conventions, skill files for the research pipeline, and rules that enforce methodological rigour in AI-assisted analysis.
HD-4007: A complete .claude workspace for security teams — covers vulnerability scanning workflows, security audit documentation, compliance checking, CVE triage, and incident response. Includes CLAUDE.md with security conventions, skill files for common security tasks, and rules that enforce security-first analysis in every AI-assisted workflow.
HD-4008: A complete .claude workspace for QA teams — covers test case generation, bug triage, regression detection, and test report writing. Includes CLAUDE.md with QA conventions, skill files for test generation and bug documentation, and rules that keep Claude producing structured, reproducible test artefacts.
HD-5001: A structured data processing record template for AI agent systems under GDPR — covers processing purposes, legal bases, data categories, retention periods, and international transfers. Pre-filled with common agent data flows so you can customise rather than start from scratch. Pairs well with the Agent Compliance and Audit Trail Framework.
HD-5002: A mapping document that aligns common AI agent capabilities to SOC 2 Trust Service Criteria — covers Security, Availability, Confidentiality, Processing Integrity, and Privacy. Includes an evidence template for each control area and notes on what agent logs, access controls, and audit trails need to exist to satisfy each criterion.
HD-5003: A structured ethical review framework for AI agent deployments — covers fairness, bias risk, transparency, accountability, and human oversight. Includes a scoring rubric and a sign-off checklist. Use it before deploying any agent that makes decisions affecting real users.
HD-5004: A step-by-step incident response playbook for when an AI agent behaves unexpectedly — covers detection criteria, immediate containment steps, investigation procedure, communication templates, remediation workflow, and post-mortem format. Designed for operators who need a response plan ready before an incident, not scrambling to build one during.
HD-5005: A role-based access control matrix for multi-agent systems — maps agent roles to data, tools, and actions with explicit allow/deny columns. Pre-structured for common agent architectures with guidance on how to extend it for your specific topology. Drop it in as the governance layer of a new deployment.
HD-5006: A data retention policy template for AI agent systems — covers retention schedules by data type, automated purge triggers, archival workflows, and GDPR deletion obligations. Pre-filled with sensible defaults for common agent data categories so you're customising, not writing from scratch.
HD-5007: A detailed specification for what AI agent systems should log — covers mandatory event types, the log schema (fields, formats, timestamps), retention requirements by event category, and the review cadence for a defensible audit trail. Includes a worked example log and a checklist for validating that a logging implementation meets the spec.
BDL-001: Four foundational guides covering alignment architecture, SOUL.md creation, autonomous agent security, and the three-layer memory model — the core reading for setting up a first serious agent operation. Each guide is a standalone download. At under $5 per guide, it's the lowest-cost path from zero to a well-grounded agent setup.
BDL-002: Combines the Operator Kit v1.0, Claude Code SDLC Pipeline, and the full Guardrails, Skill Arsenal, and Command Layer into a single package — everything needed to run Claude Code as a professional development platform. Individual product value is $406; the bundle saves $207. Aimed at developers who are past the tutorial phase and want a production-grade Claude Code setup.
BDL-003: Four products covering the full security lifecycle for AI agent systems — Security Audit Report Template, SOC2 Control Mapping, Agent Access Control Matrix, and AI Ethics Review Template. Covers audit documentation, regulatory alignment, access control design, and ethical review in one purchase. Built for security professionals and operators who need to demonstrate compliance.
BDL-004: Four products for validating and launching an AI product — Idea Validator Engine, Willingness to Pay framework, Subdomain-Based Testing Architecture, and AI Ethics Review Template. Covers the full pre-launch validation stack: idea validation, pricing research, variant testing, and ethics clearance. For founders who want to ship with evidence, not hope.
BDL-005: Five products for designing and governing complex multi-agent systems — 4-Level Authority Framework, Multi-Agent Hierarchy Decision Framework, Multi-Agent Architecture SOP, and two supporting AGENTS.md templates. Individual product value is $617; the bundle saves $218. Built for architects designing systems with real governance requirements, not just chaining a few agents together.
SVC-001: Scores any agent output against the seven Hive Doctrine alignment principles and returns a 0-100 score with a per-principle breakdown. A quick alignment sanity check you can run before shipping an agent output or as part of an automated evaluation pipeline.
SVC-002: Evaluates a system prompt across five dimensions — clarity, safety, effectiveness, specificity, and robustness — and returns an A-F grade with dimension-level comments. Use it to get a structured second opinion on a system prompt before deploying it.
SVC-003: Analyses token distribution across a prompt or context, identifies waste zones, and returns an optimised version with commentary on what was removed and why. Run it on any bloated prompt before paying for the tokens at scale.
SVC-004: Generates five culturally-rooted agent names with backstories and SOUL.md identity snippets for each. Use it when you need a named agent persona that feels grounded rather than arbitrary.
SVC-005: Converts any project README into a complete AGENTS.md file — including agent role definitions, coordination patterns, and common anti-patterns extracted from the project structure. Returns a ready-to-edit AGENTS.md that gives Claude Code immediate project context.
SVC-006: Semantic search across 270+ Hive Doctrine knowledge products — returns the most relevant context blocks with metadata and relevance scores. Use it to surface applicable patterns and frameworks from the catalogue before building something from scratch.
SVC-007: Audit of an agent configuration across 10 dimensions — security, alignment, capability coverage, tool configuration, memory setup, authority scope, compliance posture, observability, error handling, and operational readiness. Returns a scored report with specific, actionable recommendations for each dimension.
SVC-008: Generates a STRIDE-based threat model from a system description — returns attack vectors categorised by threat type, severity ratings, and concrete mitigations for each. Run it at the design stage rather than after an incident.
SVC-009: Designs a complete multi-agent architecture from a requirements description — returns agent roles, tool assignments, memory strategy, coordination patterns, and infrastructure recommendations. A starting architecture you can refine rather than a blank page.
SVC-010: Generates a complete SOUL.md constitution file from a name, role description, and personality traits — returns a fully populated identity document with cultural grounding and depth. Use it as a starting point when building a new agent persona.
SVC-011: Decomposes a complex task into a multi-agent execution plan — returns agent roles, task assignments, handoff protocols, and coordination sequence. Use it to plan multi-agent work before committing to an architecture.
SVC-012: Scans a system prompt for security vulnerabilities — checks for injection vectors, data leakage paths, privilege escalation opportunities, and output manipulation risks. Returns findings by severity with remediation guidance.
SVC-013: Estimates monthly operating costs for an agent configuration — covers LLM API, infrastructure, storage, and monitoring, with assumptions documented. Returns a cost breakdown you can stress-test by adjusting usage parameters.
SVC-014: Designs the optimal memory architecture for a described agent — returns context window strategy, persistence layer recommendation, retrieval pattern, and compression approach. Tailored to the agent's use case rather than a generic recommendation.
SVC-015: Audits MCP tool definitions or function calling schemas — checks for quality, security exposure, schema completeness, and coverage gaps. Returns findings for each tool with specific improvement suggestions.
SVC-016: Checks an agent configuration against EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001, GDPR, and SOC 2 requirements — returns a per-framework compliance gap analysis. Use it as a preliminary assessment to prioritise compliance work.
SVC-017: Generates a deployment plan for an agent system — returns infrastructure recommendations (Docker, serverless, edge, or hybrid), configuration steps, cost estimates, and a pre-deployment checklist. Tailored to the stated constraints and requirements.
SVC-018: AI-generated plausibility assessment of factual claims — returns a hedged verdict, confidence score, and notes on where the claim is uncertain or contested. A fast first-pass check, not a substitute for authoritative fact-checking.
SVC-019: Compares an agent output against provided source material and flags where claims diverge from or go beyond the source — returns a faithfulness score with annotated discrepancies. Sonnet-powered analysis.
SVC-020: AI-generated analysis of MCP tool definitions, OpenAPI specs, JSON schemas, or agent cards — returns potential issues, inconsistencies, and improvement suggestions. A fast schema review before publication or deployment.
SVC-021: Heuristic analysis of x402 endpoints and MCP server configurations — returns a risk flag summary based on pattern matching against known scam indicators. A preliminary signal only, not a definitive security assessment.
SVC-022: Returns LLM inference parameter recommendations — temperature, top-p, max tokens, stop sequences — based on the stated task type and model. Saves the iterative tuning process when setting up a new agent configuration.
SVC-023: Automated regulatory landscape scan for an agent use case and data context — identifies the regulatory frameworks potentially applicable and their key requirements. Not legal advice; use it to scope what legal advice you actually need.
SVC-024: Automated budget and safety analysis for a proposed agent action — returns a risk assessment with a go/no-go signal based on the stated budget, authority, and safety parameters. A preliminary check only; always have a human in the loop for consequential actions.
SVC-025: Converts a REST API spec (OpenAPI or described endpoint list) into MCP tool definitions and a server scaffold — returns a ready-to-review server file with tool registration, schema, and handler stubs. Review the output before deploying; generated code requires validation.
SVC-026: Analyses a set of content samples for brand voice consistency — returns a consistency score, identified voice drift patterns, and specific suggestions for tightening brand alignment. A fast qualitative audit before a content review.
SVC-027: Returns optimised variants of a prompt targeting cost, quality, or speed — includes commentary on what was changed and why. Test the variants before shipping; don't adopt the output without evaluation.
SVC-028: Preliminary scan of Terms of Service for clauses relevant to AI agent use — flags sections on automated access, data use, intellectual property, and liability. Not legal advice; use it to identify which sections to take to counsel.
SVC-029: Maps a natural language query to the most relevant Hive Doctrine services or products — returns ranked routing suggestions with relevance explanations. Use it when you have a specific problem and want to find the right tool quickly.
